rows.v: Scoring by carrying or catching the ball across the opponent's goal line, worth six points — the game's primary objective.
He stretched the ball over the pylon for the touchdown as he fell out of bounds, and the replay official spent two minutes confirming what the crowd already knew.
From the earlier rugby-style game, where a player had to literally 'touch the ball down' on the ground in the end zone — a requirement American football later dropped.
The signals a quarterback shouts before the snap — the 'snap count' — to start the play and to disguise or adjust it.
He barked the snap count, the rhythmic 'hut-hut' freezing the defense for the half-second his line needed to fire off the ball.
The 'count' refers to the cadence quarterbacks use; a hard count tries to bait the defense into jumping offside before the ball is even snapped.
The area inside the opponent's 20-yard line, where the field shrinks and scoring chances — and pressure — intensify.
They moved the ball easily until the red zone, where the compressed field strangled their offense and forced a frustrating field goal instead of seven.
Named for the 'red' danger area near the goal line. The term became a broadcasting and analytics staple for measuring a team's scoring efficiency.
A change of play called by the quarterback at the line of scrimmage after reading the defense, overriding the original call.
He saw the blitz coming, called an audible, and the offense shifted to a quick pass that punished the defense for showing its hand.
From 'audible,' meaning able to be heard — the quarterback announces the new play aloud. The famous 'Omaha!' calls were modern audibles in action.
A fake handoff designed to freeze the defense and draw it toward the run, opening receivers downfield for a pass.
The play-action fake sucked the linebackers forward, and the tight end slipped behind them wide open for an easy gain.
Descriptive — the quarterback mimics the 'action' of a running play. Its effectiveness depends entirely on a credible running game to sell the fake.
A short pass to a back or receiver behind a wall of blockers, designed to slow an aggressive pass rush.
With the rush bearing down, he dumped a screen pass to the running back, who turned upfield behind three blockers for a surprising chunk of yards.
Named because the blockers form a 'screen' in front of the receiver. It's the classic counter to a defense that rushes the passer too hard.
Physically handing the ball to a running back, the most basic way to start a running play.
The quarterback turned and extended the hand-off, the back hitting the hole at full speed before the defense could even diagnose the run.
Plainly descriptive — the ball is 'handed off' from one player to another. It's the foundation of the ground game, as old as the sport itself.
A fast, no-huddle offense run late in a half to score quickly against the clock, often managing time-outs and the sideline.
Down four with ninety seconds left, he orchestrated the two-minute drill flawlessly, stopping the clock and marching the team the length of the field.
Named for the urgency triggered by the two-minute warning near each half's end. A quarterback's command of it is a measure of true poise.
The protected area formed by the offensive line behind the line of scrimmage, where the quarterback stands to pass.
He stepped up calmly in the pocket as the walls collapsed around him, buying one more second to find his receiver downfield.
Named for the 'pocket' of space the blockers form around the passer. A 'pocket passer' is one who thrives there rather than scrambling.
Tackling the quarterback behind the line of scrimmage before they can throw, a major defensive win.
The edge rusher beat his man cleanly and buried the quarterback for a nine-yard sack, the kind of play that flips a drive on its head.
Popularized by Hall of Fame defender Deacon Jones, who coined the term, comparing the act of dropping a quarterback to sacking and looting a city.
A pass caught by a defender, immediately turning the ball over to their team — a 'pick.'
He read the quarterback's eyes the whole way, broke on the route, and the interception was a gift the offense will regret all week.
From 'to intercept,' to seize something in transit. The defensive equivalent of a quarterback's worst nightmare, instantly reversing possession.
Sending extra defenders — beyond the usual front — to rush the quarterback, gambling pressure against leaving receivers open.
The defense dialed up an all-out blitz, six rushers crashing in, betting they could get home before the quarterback could find the man they'd left uncovered.
From the German 'blitzkrieg' ('lightning war'), capturing the sudden, overwhelming nature of the rush. The borrowing entered football in the mid-20th century.
A fourth-down play near the goal line — the offense's final attempt to score before turning the ball over.
Fourth and goal from the one, season on the line; they went for it, the whole stadium standing, and the play decided everything.
Descriptive — it's fourth down with the 'goal' line as the target. The decision to 'go for it' here is among the most scrutinized in coaching.
How defenders guard receivers — man coverage (one-on-one) or zone coverage (guarding areas) — to stop the pass.
The disguised coverage fooled him completely; he expected man, threw into what he thought was open space, and a zone defender was waiting.
From 'to cover,' to guard or defend. The cat-and-mouse of disguising coverage before the snap is one of football's deepest strategic chess matches.
Losing control of the ball while carrying it, making it a live ball that either team can recover. A critical turnover.
The ball popped loose on the hit, a fumble bouncing free in the chaos, and a mad scramble of bodies dove after it as the whole game hung in the balance.
From 'to fumble,' to handle clumsily. It's one of two turnover types alongside the interception, and a strip-fumble forced by a defender is a prized play.
A rare two-point score for the defense, awarded when the ball-carrier is tackled in their own end zone.
They pinned the offense deep and sacked the quarterback in his own end zone for a safety — two points and the ball, a brutal double blow.
Named from the old rules where a team 'downed' the ball in its own end zone for 'safety.' It is the only score worth exactly two points by the defense.
Kicking the ball through the uprights for three points, usually attempted on fourth down within range.
The kicker drilled the 52-yard field goal as time expired, the ball splitting the uprights and the entire bench sprinting onto the field.
Descriptive — a goal scored from the 'field' of play by kicking, as opposed to a touchdown. Worth three points, it's the consolation prize of a stalled drive.
A short, deliberately bouncy kickoff aimed to travel just past the required distance so the kicking team can recover it.
Trailing late, they gambled on an onside kick, the ball squirting forward off the tee in a desperate scramble of diving bodies.
Named because the kicking team tries to recover a ball that is 'onside' (legally recoverable) after it travels ten yards. A high-risk comeback tactic.
Kicking the ball away to the opponent on fourth down when scoring is unlikely, to flip field position.
Facing fourth and long, they sent out the punter, who boomed a high spiral that pinned the offense deep and quietly won the field-position battle.
From 'to punt,' to kick a dropped ball before it hits the ground — a term shared with rugby. 'To punt' has even entered everyday English meaning to defer a decision.
A kick worth one point attempted after a touchdown, sending the ball through the uprights from short range. The 'PAT.'
After the touchdown, the routine extra point made it a two-score game, the kind of automatic kick nobody notices until it's somehow missed.
Officially the 'point after touchdown' (PAT), a near-automatic kick that the league moved farther back to make it less of a formality.
Catching a kickoff or punt and running it back upfield, occasionally all the way for a touchdown.
He fielded the kick at the goal line, found a seam in the coverage, and the kick-return became a 100-yard sprint that brought the house down.
Descriptive — the receiving team 'returns' the kick. A return for a touchdown is one of the most electrifying and momentum-swinging plays in the sport.
Choosing to run or pass into the end zone after a touchdown for two points instead of kicking the one-point extra point.
Down by two late, they spurned the tie and went for the two-point conversion, the whole season riding on one play from the two-yard line.
Adopted by the NFL in 1994 (and earlier in college), the option added a strategic layer, turning the routine point-after into a genuine gamble.
Illegally impeding a receiver's chance to catch the ball before it arrives, often a costly, game-changing penalty.
The flag flew for pass interference deep downfield, gifting the offense forty yards in a single call and instantly putting them in scoring range.
From 'to interfere,' to obstruct. Because the penalty can move the ball to the spot of the foul, it's among the most impactful and debated calls in the game.
A penalty for hitting the quarterback late, too high, too low, or with excessive force after the ball is thrown.
The defender's hit landed a half-second late, and the roughing-the-passer flag erased the sack and handed the offense a fresh first down.
Named for treating the passer 'roughly' beyond the rules. The protections have expanded over the years amid growing concern for player safety.
Illegally grabbing or restraining an opponent who doesn't have the ball, most commonly by a blocker holding a rusher.
The big gain came back on a holding call, the lineman caught with a fistful of jersey, and the groans rolled down from the stands.
From 'to hold,' to grasp or restrain. A running joke says holding could be called on nearly every play; officials flag only the holds that clearly affect it.
An offensive penalty when a player moves illegally before the snap, jumping early and stopping the play.
The crowd noise rattled the rookie tackle into a false start, the flat penalty wiping out the snap and pushing the offense five yards backward.
Named for a 'false' or premature start, borrowed from track and field's term for an early leap off the blocks before the gun.
A penalty when a defender is across the line of scrimmage when the ball is snapped — beaten to the punch by the count.
Lured by a hard count, the lineman jumped offsides, and the free play let the quarterback take a deep shot with nothing to lose.
Descriptive — the player is on the wrong 'side' of the line at the snap. A defender drawn offsides by a clever cadence is a small triumph for the offense.
A serious penalty (especially in college) for hitting a defenseless player in the head or neck, or leading with the crown of the helmet.
The targeting call ejected him for the rest of the game, a strict modern rule that prioritizes the safety of the head above all else.
A 21st-century rule born from the growing understanding of head injuries, designed to remove dangerous head-hunting hits from the sport entirely.
A nickname for the football field, and by extension the sport itself, from the field's old grid of lines.
Friday nights on the gridiron meant the whole town under the lights, the field's painted grid the stage for every local legend.
Named because early fields were marked with a checkerboard grid of lines that resembled a 'gridiron,' a metal grate used for cooking over a fire.
A long, desperate pass thrown toward the end zone as time expires, hoping a receiver comes down with it against the odds.
He heaved a Hail Mary into a crowd in the end zone as the clock hit zero, and somehow, impossibly, his receiver came down with it.
From the Catholic prayer; the term was famously cemented by quarterback Roger Staubach, who said he 'closed his eyes and said a Hail Mary' on a winning 1975 throw.
An affectionate slang term for the football itself.
He'd been tossing the pigskin in the backyard since he could walk, dreaming of the day a stadium would roar at his every throw.
A historical misnomer — footballs were made of leather (often cowhide) and inflated pig or animal bladders, never literally pigskin, but the nickname stuck.
An interception returned all the way for a touchdown — a 'pick' (interception) worth six points.
He jumped the route for the pick-six, racing untouched to the end zone, a fourteen-point swing in the blink of an eye.
A modern portmanteau of 'pick' (interception) and 'six' (the points for a touchdown). It captures the single most demoralizing play a quarterback can suffer.
An offensive formation where the quarterback lines up several yards behind center to receive a longer snap, gaining time to pass.
They came out in the shotgun on third and long, the quarterback set deep with extra time and space to survey the field before the rush arrived.
Named for spraying receivers wide like a shotgun's scatter, the formation was popularized in the 1960s and is now a staple of pass-first offenses.
A fast-paced offense that skips the huddle to snap plays quickly, denying the defense time to rest or substitute.
The hurry-up offense had the defense gasping, lining up exhausted play after play with no chance to swap in fresh legs.
Descriptive — the offense literally 'hurries up' between plays. Run as a constant scheme, it becomes the 'no-huddle' offense designed to wear defenses down.
Slang for the area along the line of scrimmage where the offensive and defensive linemen battle — the 'trench warfare' of football.
Games are won in the trenches, the coach always said, where unglamorous giants grind against each other on every single snap.
A wartime metaphor — the linemen's brutal, close-quarters struggle resembles the trench warfare of World War I, hidden in plain sight on every play.
